content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

August 25, 2017

Sherri Howard
Associate Engineer
City of Carlsbad Public Works
Transportation Engineering
1635 Faraday Ave.
Carlsbad, CA 92008

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-081

Dear Ms. Howard:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding Government Code Section 1090, et
seq.¹ Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or
common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and
accurate.

When providing advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Diego County
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a
written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that,
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

Whether an independent contractor hired by Carlsbad Municipal Water District to provide
design-build and short-term maintenance services for a removal and restoration project, Habitat
Restoration Sciences, Inc., is prohibited under Section 1090 from contracting with the District again
to perform long-term maintenance on three original mitigation sites.?

CONCLUSION

No. Where Habitat Restoration Sciences provided technical expertise in designing, building,
and short-term maintenance services for the removal and restoration project and did not exert
considerable influence over the decisions of the District to conduct long-term maintenance, Section
1090 is inapplicable.

¹ Government Code Sections 1090 through 1097.5. All statutory references are to the Government Code,
unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110
through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of
the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-081
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are an associate engineer at the Carlsbad Municipal Water District ("CMWD"). The CMWD is a public agency organized under the Municipal Water Act of 1911 and a subsidiary of the City of Carlsbad.

The CMWD contracted with full-service consulting firm, LSA Associates, Inc. (LSA), to create a conceptual mitigation plan for perpetual, routine maintenance of the Lake Calavera Dam.² In October 2015, LSA tendered the conceptual mitigation plan to you as representative of the CMWD. The plan detailed the construction, mitigation work, planting, monitoring, and short and long-term maintenance needed on the ongoing Calavera Dam Routine Maintenance Project ("Project").

The Routine Maintenance Project involves vegetation removal at the dam spillway and dam face per project permits and the Maintenance and Monitoring Manual for Lake Calavera Dam. As mitigation for the environmental impacts associated with removing native vegetation from the dam spillway, compensatory mitigation is required. Due to the needed mitigation, the CMWD contemplated an ancillary project – the Calavera Preserve Palm Grove Removal and Native Plant Restoration Project ("removal and restoration project").³

In June 2016, the CMWD required consulting services for the removal and restoration project. As such, it entered into a design-build contract with native habitat landscape installation and maintenance consultant, Habitat Restoration Sciences ("HRS"). CMWD incorporated LSA's conceptual mitigation plan as an exhibit in the design-build contract in order to establish the procedure. As a de facto subcontractor, HRS adhered to LSA's mitigation plan in their design, build, and maintenance for the removal and restoration project.⁴ The goal of the removal and restoration project was to establish "native riparian scrub species to the point that nonnative species [were] excluded and the native species [become] self-sustaining without human intervention." LSA's plan required HRS to perform final design and construction services to install three mitigation sites and perform 120 days of plant establishment maintenance.

The design-build contract encompassed both the design and construction phases, as well as the 120-day plant establishment maintenance phase. HRS' contractual scope of services consisted of project administration and management, final design services (including preparation of construction plans, a construction cost estimate, and technical specifications), and installation of three mitigation sites (including weed/exotic plant eradication, soil testing, site preparation, installation of cuttings and container plants, seed application, planting of plant materials, and irrigation). Additionally, the 120-day plant establishment maintenance period required further weed

² The California Division of Dams permit associated with the maintenance of the Lake Calavera Dam requires that the routine maintenance be in perpetuity.

³ A "palm grove" is a group of palm trees growing close together.

⁴ The design-build contract labels LSA's Conceptual Mitigation Plan Calavera Dam Routine Maintenance Project as a "Project background document." HRS did not participate in the development of LSA's conceptual mitigation plan.

File No. A-17-081
Page No. 3

control, pest control, fertilization, pruning and leaf litter, and removal of trash and debris. The total
area of the restoration/enhancement site was approximately 0.773 of an acre, while the mitigation
sites encompass 2.842 acres. The total mitigation effort measures 3.630 acres.

The design-build contract required that removal of the palm grove and installation of the
mitigation planting occur under the direction of LSA to the satisfaction of the California
Department of Fish and Wildlife (CDFW) pursuant to its enumerated performance standards.5 LSA
monitored all work and directed the maintenance activities, including monitoring of the site
preparation and nonnative vegetation removal, plant and seed installation, and the 120-day plant
establishment period. LSA had discretion to accept HRS' work product "upon satisfactory
completion." Moreover, LSA retained the right to determine final acceptance once HRS had planted
all plant materials in accordance with the plan specifications and the plants had demonstrated
acceptable health and growth. Ultimately, all work was subject to review under permit authorization
by the CMWD, the City of Carlsbad, and the CDFW.6

During each monitoring site visit, LSA would document the general condition of the
mitigation sites and evaluate it for compliance with the maintenance specifications and progress
toward achieving the performance standards. The results of the site visits were summarized in field
memoranda submitted to the City and HRS following each site visit. LSA finalized the memoranda
and incorporated it into the acceptance report submitted to the City and HRS.

HRS satisfactorily performed all design-build and 120-day plant establishment maintenance
services pursuant to the three phases of the design-build contract. In exchange, CMWD
compensated it to the sum of $31,000. However, there is still a need for the long-term maintenance
portion of the removal and restoration project.7

In February 2017, HRS submitted a proposal for the palm grove restoration long-term
maintenance to the City based on the conceptual restoration plan by LSA and as-built documents by
HRS. HRS proposed to perform maintenance services at the mitigation sites over a four-year period
for $94,300. The proposal specifies that HRS would perform weed control, irrigation
repairs/adjustments, erosion control repairs/replacement, trash removal, and eight watering events.

CMWD intends to award the contract to HRS given the company's history and knowledge
of the removal and restoration project.8 However, you inquire whether doing so would violate
Section 1090's prohibition against financial interest in governmental contracts.

5 The design-build contract contemplated that long-term maintenance would be required for the mitigation sites
following completion of the installation. However, it stated that the "Five-Year Maintenance Program is not a part of
this contract."

6 A permit was required from the CDFW to perform the removal of the native vegetation. HRS did not
participate in obtaining and negotiating permits.

7 Long-term maintenance tasks include weed, trash, and debris removal and any feasible efforts necessary for
the mitigation sites to meet the performance standards.

8 Since HRS proposed services will not exceed the annual threshold of $30,000 per contract year, CMWD is
not required to solicit three proposals prior to awarding the professional services contract. (See Carlsbad Municipal
Code §§ 3.28.050(A)(1) and 3.28.060(2).)

File No. A-17-081
Page No. 4

ANALYSIS

Section 1090:

Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district,
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract
made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members."

Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests,
that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering
the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is
intended "not only to strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of
impropriety." (City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey (4th DCA 1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.)

Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has
a financial interest." (People v. Honig (3d DCA 1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that
violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Id. at 646-649.)

For purposes of corporate consultants and independent contractors, the court has repeatedly
determined that Section 1090 applies to consultants who have "a hand in designing and developing
the plans and specifications" for a public project. (McGee v. Balfour Beatty Construction, LLC. (2d
DCA 2016) 247 Cal.App.4th 235, 248-249; see also Davis v. Fresno Unified School District (5th
DCA 2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 261, 301 and Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton
(2d DCA 2010) 186 Cal.App.4th 1114, 1119-20.) Accordingly, Section 1090 is generally applicable
to contractors that serve in advisory positions and have the potential to exert considerable influence
over the contracting decisions of a public agency. (See Green Advice Letter, No. A-16-084; see
also Chadwick Advice Letter, No. A-15-147 citing Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc., 186
Cal.App.4th, 1124-25.) This application recognizes that contractors, who serve the public
temporarily, still "owe the same fealty expected from permanent officers and employees." (46
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 74 (1965).)

Further guiding our analysis of whether Section 1090 applies, we have previously found that
the focus is on the scope of influence the contractor holds in advising the public entity. (See
Chadwick, p. 12.) The Second District Court of Appeals explained, "an individual's status as an
official… turns on the extent to which the person influences an agency's contracting decisions or
otherwise acts in a capacity that demands the public trust." (Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc.,
186 Cal.App.4th at 1125.) Consulting firms hired by governmental agencies may be "employees"
subject to the Section 1090 conflict of interest prohibition where they have influence over an
official decision. (See Davis, 237 Cal.App.4th 261, 300-01.)

Sub-consultants who play a limited technical role, for and through a design
consultant/advisor, such that they are removed from influencing the contracting decisions of a
public agency, may not be subject to Section 1090. (See Chadwick, supra, p. 12.) In Chadwick,
while the primary consultants and a "highly involved sub-consultant" were found to be subject to
Section 1090, the sub-consultants who provided technical input, reports, and similar information in

File No. A-17-081
Page No. 5

a support role to the consultants, were not subject to Section 1090. These sub-consultants were
sufficiently removed from directly advising the city staff, and did not exert considerable influence
over the contracting decisions. (Ibid.)

In this case, HRS was hired to design and construct a removal and restoration project
ancillary to the ongoing Calavera Dam Routine Maintenance Project. HRS' design-build services
operated as a form of compensatory mitigation for the environmental impacts associated with the
ongoing Project. LSA solely comprised the conceptual mitigation plan, detailing the construction,
mitigation work, planting, monitoring, and short and long-term maintenance needed for the Project.
The compensatory mitigation plan was then used as the blueprint for the removal and restoration
project.

Although HRS played more than a "limited technical role" in designing and constructing the
removal and restoration project, it merely follows LSA's instructions. Moreover, all of HRS' work
product was subject to LSA's supervision and approval throughout the entire removal and
restoration project. It does not appear that HRS acted in a role of "exerting considerable influence"
over the CMWD decision-making process in structuring the logistics of the palm grove restoration
long-term maintenance. In this regard, HRS has not exerted considerable influence over the
CMWD's decision to contract for the long-term maintenance, and is thus not subject to Section
1090.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack Woodside
General Counsel

By:    Ryan P. O'Connor
       Counsel, Legal Division

RPOC:jgl

c9nsiderable influence over the decisions of the District t<;t conduct long-term maintenance, Section

sections.analysis:
Section l 090:  
Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, 
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract 
made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members." 
Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, 
that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering 
the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is 
intended "not only to strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of 
impropriety." (City of Imperial Beac/1 v. Bailey (4th DCA 1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest." (People v. Honig (3d DCA 1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that 
violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies 
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Id. at 646-649.) 
For purposes of corporate consultants and independent contractors, the court has repeatedly 
determined that Section 1090 applies to consultants who have "a hand in designing and developing 
the plans and specifications" for a public project. (McGee , .. Balfour Beatty Construction, LLC, (2d 
DCA 2016) 247 Cal.App.4th 235, 248-249; see also Davis v. Fresno Unified Sc/zoo/ District (5th 
DCA 2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 261,301 and Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton 
(2d DCA 2010) 186 Cal.App.4th 1114, 1119-20.) Accordingly, Section 1090 is generally applicable 
to contractors that serve in advisory positions and have the potential to exert considerable influence 
over the contracting decisions of a public agency. (See Green Advice Letter, No. A-16-084; see 
also Chadwick Advice Letter, No. A-15-147 citing Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc., 186

temporarily, still "owe the same fealty expected from permanent officers and employees." (46

Further guiding our analysis of whether Section 1090 applies, we have previously found that 
the focus is on the scope of influence the contractor holds in advising the public entity. (See

official decision. (See Davis, 237 Cal.App.4th 261, 300-01.)

consultant/advisor, such that they are removed from influencing the contracting decisions of a

while the primary consultants and a "highly involved sub-consultant" were found to be subject to 
Section l 090, the sub-consultants who provided technical input, reports, and similar information in  
a support role to the consultants, were not subject to Section 1090. These sub-consultants were 
sufficiently removed from directly advising the city staff, and did not exert considerable influence 
over the contracting decisions. (Ibid.) 
In this case, HRS was hired to design and construct a removal and restoration project 
ancillary to the ongoing Calavera Dam Routine Maintenance Project. HRS' design-build services 
operated as a fonn of compensatory mitigation for the environmental impacts associated with the 
ongoing Project. LSA solely composed the conceptual mitigation plan, detailing the construction, 
mitigation work, planting, monitoring, and short and long-tenn maintenance needed for the Project. 
The compensatory mitigation plan was then used as the blueprint for the removal and restoration 
project. 
Although HRS played more than a "limited technical role" in designing and constructing the 
removal and restoration project, it merely followed LSA's instructions. Moreover, all of HRS' work 
product was subject to LSA' s supervision and approval throughout the entire removal and 
restoration project. It does not appear that HRS acted in a role of "exerting considerable influence" 
over the CMWD decision-making process in structuring the logistics of the palm grove restoration
